solution:
Such a set exists for every $n$. To construct an example, define the function $f: \mathbb{Z}^n \to \mathbb{Z}/(2n+1) \mathbb{Z}$ by
\[
f(x_1,\dots,x_n) = x_1 + 2x_2 + \cdots + nx_n \pmod{2n+1},
\]
then let $S$ be the preimage of 0.

To check condition (1), note that if $p \in S$ and $q$ is a neighbor of $p$ differing only in coordinate $i$, then
\[
f(q) = f(p) \pm i \equiv \pm i \pmod{2n+1}
\]
and so $q \notin S$.

To check condition (2), note that if $p \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ is not in $S$, then there exists a unique choice of $i \in \{1,\dots,n\}$ such that $f(p)$ is congruent to one of $+i$ or $-i$ modulo $2n+1$. The unique neighbor $q$ of $p$ in $S$ is then obtained by either subtracting $1$ from, or adding $1$ to, the $i$-th coordinate of $p$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
According to Art of Problem Solving (thread c6h366290), this problem was a 1985 IMO submission from Czechoslovakia. For an application to steganography, see:
J. Fridrich and P. Lison\v{e}k, Grid colorings in steganography,
\textit{IEEE Transactions on Information Theory} \textbf{53} (2007), 1547--1549.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long.solution:
Such a set exists for every $dimension$. To construct an example, define the function $mapping: \mathbb{Z}^{dimension} \to \mathbb{Z}/(2 dimension +1) \mathbb{Z}$ by
\[
mapping(coordone,\dots,coordn) = coordone + 2coordtwo + \cdots + dimension\,coordn \pmod{2 dimension + 1},
\]
then let $pointset$ be the preimage of 0.

To check condition (1), note that if $pointone \in pointset$ and $pointtwo$ is a neighbor of $pointone$ differing only in coordinate $indexer$, then
\[
mapping(pointtwo) = mapping(pointone) \pm indexer \equiv \pm indexer \pmod{2 dimension + 1}
\]
and so $pointtwo \notin pointset$.

To check condition (2), note that if $pointone \in \mathbb{Z}^{dimension}$ is not in $pointset$, then there exists a unique choice of $indexer \in \{1,\dots,dimension\}$ such that $mapping(pointone)$ is congruent to one of $+indexer$ or $-indexer$ modulo $2 dimension + 1$. The unique neighbor $pointtwo$ of $pointone$ in $pointset$ is then obtained by either subtracting $1$ from, or adding $1$ to, the $indexer$-th coordinate of $pointone$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
According to Art of Problem Solving (thread c6h366290), this problem was a 1985 IMO submission from Czechoslovakia. For an application to steganography, see:
J. Fridrich and P. Lison\v{e}k, Grid colorings in steganography,
\textit{IEEE Transactions on Information Theory} \textbf{53} (2007), 1547--1549.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Such a set exists for every $dragonfly$. To construct an example, define the function $windshield: \mathbb{Z}^{dragonfly} \to \mathbb{Z}/(2\dragonfly+1) \mathbb{Z}$ by
\[
windshield(waterfall,\dots,journeying) = waterfall + 2candlestick + \cdots + \dragonfly journeying \pmod{2\dragonfly+1},
\]
then let $constable$ be the preimage of 0.

To check condition (1), note that if $pineapple \in constable$ and $saxophone$ is a neighbor of $pineapple$ differing only in coordinate $marigolds$, then
\[
windshield(saxophone) = windshield(pineapple) \pm marigolds \equiv \pm marigolds \pmod{2\dragonfly+1}
\]
and so $saxophone \notin constable$.

To check condition (2), note that if $pineapple \in \mathbb{Z}^{dragonfly}$ is not in $constable$, then there exists a unique choice of $marigolds \in \{1,\dots,\dragonfly\}$ such that $windshield(pineapple)$ is congruent to one of $+marigolds$ or $-marigolds$ modulo $2\dragonfly+1$. The unique neighbor $saxophone$ of $pineapple$ in $constable$ is then obtained by either subtracting $1$ from, or adding $1$ to, the $marigolds$-th coordinate of $pineapple$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
According to Art of Problem Solving (thread c6h366290), this problem was a 1985 IMO submission from Czechoslovakia. For an application to steganography, see:
J. Fridrich and P. Lison\v{e}k, Grid colorings in steganography,
\textit{IEEE Transactions on Information Theory} \textbf{53} (2007), 1547--1549.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Such a set exists for every $dimensionless$. To construct an example, define the function $constantmap: \mathbb{Z}^{dimensionless} \to \mathbb{Z}/(2dimensionless+1) \mathbb{Z}$ by
\[
constantmap(lastplace,\dots,constantnth) = lastplace + 2\,latestsecond + \cdots + dimensionless\,constantnth \pmod{2dimensionless+1},
\]
then let $universalset$ be the preimage of 0.

To check condition (1), note that if $voidpoint \in universalset$ and $nullpoint$ is a neighbor of $voidpoint$ differing only in coordinate $targetvalue$, then
\[
constantmap(nullpoint) = constantmap(voidpoint) \pm targetvalue \equiv \pm targetvalue \pmod{2dimensionless+1}
\]
and so $nullpoint \notin universalset$.

To check condition (2), note that if $voidpoint \in \mathbb{Z}^{dimensionless}$ is not in $universalset$, then there exists a unique choice of $targetvalue \in \{1,\dots,dimensionless\}$ such that $constantmap(voidpoint)$ is congruent to one of $+targetvalue$ or $-targetvalue$ modulo $2dimensionless+1$. The unique neighbor $nullpoint$ of $voidpoint$ in $universalset$ is then obtained by either subtracting $1$ from, or adding $1$ to, the $targetvalue$-th coordinate of $voidpoint$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
According to Art of Problem Solving (thread c6h366290), this problem was a 1985 IMO submission from Czechoslovakia. For an application to steganography, see:
J. Fridrich and P. Lison\v{e}k, Grid colorings in steganography,
\textit{IEEE Transactions on Information Theory} \textbf{53} (2007), 1547--1549.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}

garbled_string.solution:
Such a set exists for every $kvorsulei$. To construct an example, define the function $hqkimavod: \mathbb{Z}^{kvorsulei} \to \mathbb{Z}/(2kvorsulei+1) \mathbb{Z}$ by
\[
 hqkimavod(ozmtpvcra,\dots,fjrakslom) = ozmtpvcra + 2levurnito + \cdots + kvorsulei fjrakslom \pmod{2kvorsulei+1},
\]
then let $pmzatgore$ be the preimage of 0.

To check condition (1), note that if $zdwqleopa \in pmzatgore$ and $buftrazik$ is a neighbor of $zdwqleopa$ differing only in coordinate $ypbmactez$, then
\[
 hqkimavod(buftrazik) = hqkimavod(zdwqleopa) \pm ypbmactez \equiv \pm ypbmactez \pmod{2kvorsulei+1}
\]
and so $buftrazik \notin pmzatgore$.

To check condition (2), note that if $zdwqleopa \in \mathbb{Z}^{kvorsulei}$ is not in $pmzatgore$, then there exists a unique choice of $ypbmactez \in \{1,\dots,kvorsulei\}$ such that $hqkimavod(zdwqleopa)$ is congruent to one of $+ypbmactez$ or $-ypbmactez$ modulo $2kvorsulei+1$. The unique neighbor $buftrazik$ of $zdwqleopa$ in $pmzatgore$ is then obtained by either subtracting $1$ from, or adding $1$ to, the $ypbmactez$-th coordinate of $zdwqleopa$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
According to Art of Problem Solving (thread c6h366290), this problem was a 1985 IMO submission from Czechoslovakia. For an application to steganography, see:
J. Fridrich and P. Lison\v{e}k, Grid colorings in steganography,
\textit{IEEE Transactions on Information Theory} \textbf{53} (2007), 1547--1549.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 1$.  Equip the integer lattice $\mathbb Z^{n}$ with the graph $\Gamma_{n}$ in which two vertices are \textbf{adjacent} when they differ by $\pm 1$ in \emph{exactly one} coordinate and are equal in all other coordinates.  

A subset $C\subset \mathbb Z^{n}$ is called a (lattice-)\textbf{$1$-perfect code} in $\Gamma_{n}$ if  

(i) (independence) no two vertices of $C$ are adjacent;  

(ii) (perfect domination) every vertex of $\mathbb Z^{n}\setminus C$ is adjacent to \emph{exactly one} vertex of $C$.

The code is \textbf{lattice-periodic} if $C$ is an affine lattice, i.e.\ $C=v+L$ for some full-rank sublattice $L\le \mathbb Z^{n}$ and some $v\in\mathbb Z^{n}$.  
Fix a lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code $C=v+L$ and form the finite abelian quotient group  

\[
G:=\mathbb Z^{n}/L ,\qquad \bar 0\in G \text{ the identity}.
\]

Whenever convenient, vectors of $\mathbb Z^{n}$ are identified with their residue classes in $G$.

A.\;(\emph{Parameter set attached to a code})  
Show that $\lvert G\rvert = 2n+1$ and that the $2n$ residue classes  

\[
\Sigma := \{\,\pm\bar e_{1},\dots ,\pm\bar e_{n}\}\subset G\setminus\{\bar 0\}
\]

exhaust $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$.  In particular the $2n$ vectors $\pm e_{i}$ are pairwise incongruent modulo $L$.

B.\;(\emph{Constructing codes from a parameter set})  
Conversely, let $G$ be an abelian group of \emph{odd} order $2n+1$ and pick elements  

\[
a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}\in G
\]

such that the multiset $\{\pm a_{1},\dots ,\pm a_{n}\}$ equals $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$.  Define the homomorphism and the kernel  

\[
\Phi:\mathbb Z^{n}\longrightarrow G,\qquad\Phi(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})=x_{1}a_{1}+\dots +x_{n}a_{n},\qquad
L:=\ker\Phi .
\]

Prove that  

(1) $L$ has index $\lvert\mathbb Z^{n}:L\rvert = 2n+1$;  

(2) every coset $v+L$ ($v\in\mathbb Z^{n}$) is a lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code in $\Gamma_{n}$.

C.\;(\emph{Classification up to signed permutations})  
Prove that every lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code in $\mathbb Z^{n}$ arises from the construction in B.  

Furthermore, let $(G,a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})$ and $(G',a_{1}',\dots ,a_{n}')$ be two data sets as in B, producing lattices $L,L'\le\mathbb Z^{n}$.  Show that  

\[
L'=P(L)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 
\exists\;\text{an isomorphism }\psi:G\;\cong\;G' 
\text{ and a signed permutation matrix }P\in \mathrm{GL}_{n}(\mathbb Z)
\text{ with }\psi(a_{i})=\pm a'_{\sigma(i)}\;\forall i,
\]

where $\sigma$ is the permutation encoded by $P$.  (A \emph{signed permutation matrix} is obtained from a permutation matrix by changing signs of arbitrary rows.)

Consequently, the set of lattices supporting $1$-perfect codes in $\mathbb Z^{n}$, taken modulo the natural action of the signed permutation group, is in bijection with the ``signed $1$-factorisations'' of the complete graph $K_{2n+1}$ (viewed as a Cayley graph of any abelian group of order $2n+1$).
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $e_{1},\dots ,e_{n}$ denotes the standard basis of $\mathbb Z^{n}$, and for an abelian group $G$ we write $\bar 0$ for its identity element.



\textbf{Part A.}

After translating by $-v$ we may suppose $0\in C$, hence $C=L$.

\emph{A1 - Size of $G$.}  
For $c\in L$ put  

\[
B_{1}(c):=\{c\}\cup\{c\pm e_{1},\dots ,c\pm e_{n}\},\qquad 
B_{1}:=B_{1}(0).
\]

Independence and perfect domination together imply that the family  
$\{B_{1}(c)\}_{c\in L}$ is a \emph{partition} of $\mathbb Z^{n}$:

* independence guarantees that distinct codewords are not adjacent, hence the balls are pairwise disjoint in their centres;  

* perfect domination guarantees that every vertex lies in some ball.

Consider the map  

\[
\iota:B_{1}\longrightarrow G,\qquad x\longmapsto\bar x .
\]

\emph{Injectivity of $\iota$.}  
Assume $x,y\in B_{1}$ and $\bar x=\bar y$, so $d:=x-y\in L$.  
Because $x,y\in B_{1}$, every coordinate of $d$ lies in $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$.

(i)\;Suppose $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}=1$.  
If $d$ has exactly one non-zero coordinate, then $d=\pm e_{k}$, so $0$ and $d$ are adjacent vertices \emph{both} in $L$, contradicting independence.  
If $d$ has at least two non-zero coordinates (all equal to $\pm1$), pick one such coordinate $k$ and set $z:=\operatorname{sgn}(d_{k})\,e_{k}$.  
Then $z$ is adjacent to $0$ (because it differs from $0$ by $\pm e_{k}$) and also to $d$ (because $d-z$ differs from $d$ by $\pm e_{k}$).  
Thus $z\notin L$ would have \emph{two} distinct neighbours in $L$, violating perfect domination.  
Hence $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}\neq 1$.

(ii)\;Suppose $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}=2$.  
Choose an index $k$ with $d_{k}=\pm 2$ and put $z:=\operatorname{sgn}(d_{k})\,e_{k}$.  
Exactly as above, $z$ would have two neighbours in $L$, contradicting perfect domination.  
Therefore $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}\neq 2$.

Since all coordinates of $d$ lie in $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$, the only remaining possibility is $d=0$, proving that $\iota$ is injective.

\emph{Surjectivity of $\iota$.}  
Given $g\in G$, choose a lift $x\in\mathbb Z^{n}$.  
Find the unique $c\in L$ with $x\in B_{1}(c)$; write $x=c+u$ with $u\in B_{1}$.  
Then $g=\bar x=\bar u=\iota(u)$, so $\iota$ is surjective.

Consequently  

\[
\lvert G\rvert=\lvert B_{1}\rvert=1+2n=2n+1.
\]

\emph{A2 - Non-triviality of the classes $\pm\bar e_{i}$.}  
If $\bar e_{i}=\bar 0$ then $e_{i}\in L=C$, contradicting independence (since $e_{i}$ is adjacent to $0$).  
The same argument shows $-\bar e_{i}\neq\bar 0$.

\emph{A3 - Exhaustion and distinctness.}  
Take $g\in G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$ and lift it to $x\in\mathbb Z^{n}$.  
As $x\notin L$, perfect domination supplies a unique index $j$ and sign $\varepsilon\in\{\pm 1\}$ for which $x-\varepsilon e_{j}\in L$.  Consequently  

\[
g=\bar x=\varepsilon\bar e_{j}\in\Sigma .
\]

Therefore $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}\subseteq\Sigma$, and since both sets have size $2n$, equality holds.  
All elements of $\Sigma$ are pairwise distinct; hence the $2n$ vectors $\pm e_{i}$ are incongruent modulo $L$.
\hfill$\square$



\textbf{Part B.  Construction from a parameter set}

The hypothesis implies that the $2n$ elements $\pm a_{i}$ are pairwise distinct.

\emph{B1 - Index of $L$.}  
Each non-zero element of $G$ is some $\pm a_{j}$ and, by definition, $\pm a_{j}=\Phi(\pm e_{j})$.  
Thus $\Phi$ is surjective and  

\[
\lvert\mathbb Z^{n}:L\rvert=\lvert G\rvert=2n+1 .
\]

\emph{B2 - Independence of $L$.}  
Let $y\in L$ and let $z$ be the vertex obtained by changing the $i$-th coordinate of $y$ by $\pm 1$.  
Then  

\[
\Phi(z)=\Phi(y)\pm a_{i}=0\pm a_{i}=\pm a_{i}\neq\bar 0 ,
\]

so $z\notin L$.  Hence no two vertices of $L$ are adjacent.

\emph{B3 - Perfect domination of every coset.}  
Fix $x\notin L$ and put $g:=\Phi(x)\neq\bar 0$.  
Write $g=\varepsilon a_{i}$ (unique by pairwise distinctness).  Define  

\[
x^{-}:=x-\varepsilon e_{i},\qquad x^{+}:=x+\varepsilon e_{i}.
\]

First,  

\[
\Phi(x^{-})=g-\varepsilon a_{i}=0\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^{-}\in L,
\]

so $x$ has at least one neighbour in $L$.

\emph{Uniqueness of that neighbour.}  
There are $2n-1$ other neighbours of $x$:

(a) the vertex $x^{+}$ obtained by moving in the \emph{same} coordinate $i$ but the opposite direction;  

(b) the $2(n-1)$ vertices obtained by moving in some other coordinate $j\neq i$.

\underline{Case (a):}  
\[
\Phi(x^{+})=g+\varepsilon a_{i}=2\varepsilon a_{i}\neq\bar 0
\]
because the order of $a_{i}$ divides $2n+1$ (an odd integer), hence $2$ is invertible in $G$ and $2\varepsilon a_{i}\neq\bar 0$.  Therefore $x^{+}\notin L$.

\underline{Case (b):}  
For a neighbour $y$ obtained by modifying coordinate $j\neq i$ we have  
\[
\Phi(y)=g\pm a_{j}=\varepsilon a_{i}\pm a_{j}\neq\bar 0,
\]
the last inequality using again the pairwise distinctness of the $2n$ elements $\pm a_{k}$. So such $y\notin L$.

Hence $x^{-}$ is the \emph{unique} neighbour of $x$ lying in $L$. Translating by any $v\in\mathbb Z^{n}$ shows that each coset $v+L$ is a lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code.
\hfill$\square$



\textbf{Part C.  Classification up to signed permutations}

\emph{C1 - Every lattice-periodic code comes from B.}  
For a given code, Part A yields $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}=\{\pm\bar e_{1},\dots ,\pm\bar e_{n}\}$.  
Taking $a_{i}:=\bar e_{i}$ and $\Phi$ as in Part B we have $\ker\Phi=L$, so the code arises from the construction.

\emph{C2 - Necessity of the stated condition.}  
Assume $L'=P(L)$ for a signed permutation matrix $P\in\mathrm{GL}_{n}(\mathbb Z)$.  
Let  

\[
\pi:\mathbb Z^{n}\twoheadrightarrow G:=\mathbb Z^{n}/L,\qquad
\pi':\mathbb Z^{n}\twoheadrightarrow G':=\mathbb Z^{n}/L'
\]

be the quotient maps corresponding to the two lattices.  
Because $P$ bijects $L$ onto $L'$,  

\[
\psi:G\longrightarrow G',\qquad\psi(\bar x):=\overline{P(x)}
\]

is a well-defined group isomorphism.  For a basis vector $e_{i}$,

\[
\psi(a_{i})=\psi\bigl(\Phi(e_{i})\bigr)=\overline{P(e_{i})}= \pm \overline{e_{\sigma(i)}}=\pm\Phi'(e_{\sigma(i)})=\pm a_{\sigma(i)}',
\]

where $\sigma$ and the signs are encoded by $P$.  This establishes the implication $L'=P(L)\Longrightarrow(\psi,P)$.

\emph{C3 - Sufficiency.}  
Conversely, suppose an isomorphism $\psi:G\cong G'$ and a signed permutation matrix $P$ satisfy $\psi(a_{i})=\pm a'_{\sigma(i)}$ for every $i$.  
Let $\Phi,\Phi'$ be the homomorphisms defined from the two data sets.  For each $e_{i}$,

\[
(\psi\circ\Phi)(e_{i})=\psi(a_{i})=\pm a_{\sigma(i)}'=\Phi'(P(e_{i})),
\]

so $\psi\circ\Phi=\Phi'\circ P$ on $\mathbb Z^{n}$.  Taking kernels gives $L=P^{-1}(L')$, whence $L'=P(L)$.

\emph{C4 - Combinatorial interpretation.}  
Partitioning $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$ into the $n$ antipodal pairs $\{\pm a_{i}\}$ is equivalent to choosing a $1$-factorisation of the Cayley graph $\operatorname{Cay}(G,G\setminus\{\bar 0\})\cong K_{2n+1}$.  
Quotienting by group automorphisms and by signed permutations of the coordinates yields the claimed bijection with ``signed $1$-factorisations'' of $K_{2n+1}$.

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Additional structural hypothesis (lattice periodicity) forces a full classification problem instead of a single construction.  
2.  The proof demands group-theoretic arguments (finite Abelian quotients, cyclicity, generators) combined with lattice index computations, not present in the original.  
3.  One must show necessity (order 2n+1, cyclic quotient, relation (*)) and sufficiency—two non-trivial directions requiring careful counting and independence/ domination arguments.  
4.  Identifying all perfect codes up to the automorphism group involves deeper insight into invariant theory (re-labelling coordinates, sign changes, translations).  
5.  The solution blends combinatorics on graphs, lattice theory, and finite group theory; each step is subtle and none can be bypassed by the direct modular trick that solves the original problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 1$.  Equip the integer lattice $\mathbb Z^{n}$ with the graph $\Gamma_{n}$ in which two vertices are adjacent when they differ by $\pm 1$ in exactly one coordinate.  
A subset $C\subset \mathbb Z^{n}$ is called a (lattice-)\textbf{$1$-perfect code} in $\Gamma_{n}$ if  

(i) (independence) no two vertices of $C$ are adjacent;  

(ii) (perfect domination) every vertex of $\mathbb Z^{n}\setminus C$ is adjacent to \emph{exactly one} vertex of $C$.

The code is \textbf{lattice-periodic} if $C$ is an affine lattice, i.e.\ $C=v+L$ for some full-rank sublattice $L\le \mathbb Z^{n}$ and some $v\in\mathbb Z^{n}$.  
Fix a lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code $C=v+L$ and form the finite abelian quotient group  

\[
G:=\mathbb Z^{n}/L ,\qquad \bar 0\in G \text{ the identity}.
\]

Whenever convenient, vectors of $\mathbb Z^{n}$ are identified with their residue classes in $G$.

A.  (Parameter set attached to a code)  
Show that $\lvert G\rvert = 2n+1$ and that the $2n$ residue classes  

\[
\Sigma := \{\,\pm\bar e_{1},\dots ,\pm\bar e_{n}\}\subset G\setminus\{\bar 0\}
\]

exhaust $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$.  In particular the $2n$ vectors $\pm e_{i}$ are pairwise incongruent modulo $L$.

B.  (Constructing codes from a parameter set)  
Conversely, let $G$ be an abelian group of \emph{odd} order $2n+1$ and pick elements  

\[
a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}\in G
\]

such that the multiset $\{\pm a_{1},\dots ,\pm a_{n}\}$ equals $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$.  Define the homomorphism and the kernel  

\[
\Phi:\mathbb Z^{n}\longrightarrow G,\qquad\Phi(x_{1},\dots ,x_{n})=x_{1}a_{1}+\dots +x_{n}a_{n},\qquad
L:=\ker\Phi .
\]

Prove that  

(1) $L$ has index $\lvert\mathbb Z^{n}:L\rvert = 2n+1$;  

(2) every coset $v+L$ ($v\in\mathbb Z^{n}$) is a lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code in $\Gamma_{n}$.

C.  (Classification up to signed permutations)  
Prove that every lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code in $\mathbb Z^{n}$ arises from the construction in B.  

Furthermore, let $(G,a_{1},\dots ,a_{n})$ and $(G',a_{1}',\dots ,a_{n}')$ be two data sets as in B, producing lattices $L,L'\le\mathbb Z^{n}$.  Show that  

\[
L'=P(L)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 
\exists\;\text{an isomorphism }\psi:G\;\cong\;G' 
\text{ and a signed permutation matrix }P\in \mathrm{GL}_{n}(\mathbb Z)
\text{ with }\psi(a_{i})=\pm a'_{\sigma(i)}\;\forall i,
\]

where $\sigma$ is the permutation encoded by $P$.  (A \emph{signed permutation matrix} is obtained from a permutation matrix by changing signs of arbitrary rows.)

Consequently, the set of lattices supporting $1$-perfect codes in $\mathbb Z^{n}$, taken modulo the natural action of the signed permutation group, is in bijection with the ``signed $1$-factorisations'' of the complete graph $K_{2n+1}$ (viewed as a Cayley graph of any abelian group of order $2n+1$).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $e_{1},\dots ,e_{n}$ denotes the standard basis of $\mathbb Z^{n}$, and for an abelian group $G$ we write $\bar 0$ for its identity element.



Part A.  

After translating by $-v$ we may suppose $0\in C$, hence $C=L$.

A1 - Size of $G$.  
For $c\in L$ put  

\[
B_{1}(c):=\{c\}\cup\{c\pm e_{1},\dots ,c\pm e_{n}\},\qquad 
B_{1}:=B_{1}(0).
\]

Independence and perfect domination together imply that the family  
$\{B_{1}(c)\}_{c\in L}$ yields a \emph{partition} of $\mathbb Z^{n}$:

* independence guarantees that distinct codewords are not adjacent, hence the balls are pairwise disjoint in their centres;  

* perfect domination guarantees that every vertex lies in some ball.

Consider the map  

\[
\iota:B_{1}\longrightarrow G,\qquad x\longmapsto\bar x .
\]

Injectivity of $\iota$.  
Assume $x,y\in B_{1}$ and $\bar x=\bar y$, so $d:=x-y\in L$.  
If $d\neq 0$, then each coordinate of $d$ lies in $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$ because both $x$ and $y$ are in $B_{1}$.  
Independence already rules out $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}=1$, so suppose $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}=2$.  
Choose an index $k$ with $d_{k}=\pm2$ and set  

\[
z:=y+\operatorname{sgn}(d_{k})\,e_{k}.
\]

Then $z$ is adjacent to \emph{both} $x$ and $y$, hence $z$ would have two distinct code-neighbours, contradicting perfect domination.  
Therefore $\lVert d\rVert_{\infty}\ge 3$.  But this is impossible since $d$ has all coordinates in $\{-2,-1,0,1,2\}$.  
Hence $d=0$ and $x=y$; $\iota$ is injective.

Surjectivity of $\iota$.  
Given $g\in G$, choose a lift $x\in\mathbb Z^{n}$.  
Find the unique $c\in L$ with $x\in B_{1}(c)$; write $x=c+u$ with $u\in B_{1}$.  
Then $g=\bar x=\bar u=\iota(u)$, so $\iota$ is surjective.

Consequently  

\[
\lvert G\rvert=\lvert B_{1}\rvert=1+2n=2n+1.
\]

A2 - Non-triviality of the classes $\pm\bar e_{i}$.  
If $\bar e_{i}=\bar 0$ then $e_{i}\in L=C$, contradicting independence (since $e_{i}$ is adjacent to $0$).  
The same argument shows $-\bar e_{i}\neq\bar 0$.

A3 - Exhaustion and distinctness.  
Take $g\in G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$ and lift it to $x\in\mathbb Z^{n}$.  
As $x\notin L$, perfect domination supplies a unique index $j$ and sign $\varepsilon\in\{\pm 1\}$ for which $x-\varepsilon e_{j}\in L$.  Consequently  

\[
g=\bar x=\varepsilon\bar e_{j}\in\Sigma .
\]

Therefore $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}\subseteq\Sigma$, and since both sets have size $2n$, equality holds.  
All elements of $\Sigma$ are pairwise distinct; hence the $2n$ vectors $\pm e_{i}$ are incongruent modulo $L$.
\hfill$\square$



Part B.  Construction from a parameter set  

The hypothesis implies that the $2n$ elements $\pm a_{i}$ are pairwise distinct.

B1 - Index of $L$.  
Each non-zero element of $G$ is some $\pm a_{j}$ and, by definition, $\pm a_{j}=\Phi(\pm e_{j})$.  
Thus $\Phi$ is surjective and  

\[
\lvert\mathbb Z^{n}:L\rvert=\lvert G\rvert=2n+1 .
\]

B2 - Independence of $L$.  
Let $y\in L$ and let $z$ be the vertex obtained by changing the $i$-th coordinate of $y$ by $\pm1$.  
Then  

\[
\Phi(z)=\Phi(y)\pm a_{i}=0\pm a_{i}=\pm a_{i}\neq\bar 0 ,
\]

so $z\notin L$.  Hence no two vertices of $L$ are adjacent.

B3 - Perfect domination of every coset.  
Fix $x\notin L$ and put $g:=\Phi(x)\neq\bar 0$.  
Write $g=\varepsilon a_{i}$ (unique by pairwise distinctness).  Define $x':=x-\varepsilon e_{i}$.  Then  

\[
\Phi(x')=g-\varepsilon a_{i}=0,
\qquad\text{so }x'\in L,
\]

showing $x$ has at least one neighbour in $L$.

If $y$ is another neighbour of $x$, obtained by modifying the coordinate $j\neq i$, then  

\[
\Phi(y)=g\pm a_{j}=\varepsilon a_{i}\pm a_{j}.
\]

Because the multiset $\{\pm a_{1},\dots ,\pm a_{n}\}$ contains $2n$ distinct elements, $\varepsilon a_{i}\pm a_{j}\neq\bar 0$, so $y\notin L$.  
Hence $x$ is adjacent to \emph{exactly one} vertex of $L$.  

Translating by any $v\in\mathbb Z^{n}$ shows that each coset $v+L$ is a lattice-periodic $1$-perfect code.
\hfill$\square$



Part C.  Classification up to signed permutations  

C1 - Every lattice-periodic code comes from B.  
For a given code, Part A yields $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}=\{\pm\bar e_{1},\dots ,\pm\bar e_{n}\}$.  
Taking $a_{i}:=\bar e_{i}$ and $\Phi$ as in Part B we have $\ker\Phi=L$, so the code arises from the construction.

C2 - Necessity of the stated condition.  
Assume $L'=P(L)$ for a signed permutation matrix $P\in\mathrm{GL}_{n}(\mathbb Z)$.  
Let  

\[
\pi:\mathbb Z^{n}\twoheadrightarrow G:=\mathbb Z^{n}/L,\qquad
\pi':\mathbb Z^{n}\twoheadrightarrow G':=\mathbb Z^{n}/L'
\]

be quotient maps.  Because $P$ bijects $L$ onto $L'$,  

\[
\psi:G\longrightarrow G',\qquad\psi(\bar x):=\overline{P(x)}
\]

is a well-defined group isomorphism.  
For a basis vector,  

\[
\psi(\bar e_{i})=\overline{P(e_{i})}= \pm \bar e_{\sigma(i)}',
\]

where $\sigma$ and the signs are encoded by $P$.  Writing $a_{i}=\bar e_{i}$ and $a_{j}'=\bar e_{j}'$ gives $\psi(a_{i})=\pm a_{\sigma(i)}'$, establishing the implication.

C3 - Sufficiency.  
Conversely, suppose an isomorphism $\psi:G\cong G'$ and a signed permutation matrix $P$ satisfy $\psi(a_{i})=\pm a'_{\sigma(i)}$ for every $i$.  
Let $\Phi,\Phi'$ be the homomorphisms defined from the two data sets.  For each $e_{i}$,

\[
(\psi\circ\Phi)(e_{i})=\psi(a_{i})=\pm a_{\sigma(i)}'=\Phi'(P(e_{i})),
\]

so $\psi\circ\Phi=\Phi'\circ P$ on $\mathbb Z^{n}$.  Taking kernels gives $L=P^{-1}(L')$, whence $L'=P(L)$.

C4 - Combinatorial interpretation.  
Partitioning $G\setminus\{\bar 0\}$ into the $n$ antipodal pairs $\{\pm a_{i}\}$ is equivalent to choosing a $1$-factorisation of the Cayley graph $\mathrm{Cay}(G,G\setminus\{\bar 0\})\cong K_{2n+1}$.  
Quotienting by group automorphisms and by signed permutations of the coordinates yields the claimed bijection with ``signed $1$-factorisations'' of $K_{2n+1}$.

\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Additional structural hypothesis (lattice periodicity) forces a full classification problem instead of a single construction.  
2.  The proof demands group-theoretic arguments (finite Abelian quotients, cyclicity, generators) combined with lattice index computations, not present in the original.  
3.  One must show necessity (order 2n+1, cyclic quotient, relation (*)) and sufficiency—two non-trivial directions requiring careful counting and independence/ domination arguments.  
4.  Identifying all perfect codes up to the automorphism group involves deeper insight into invariant theory (re-labelling coordinates, sign changes, translations).  
5.  The solution blends combinatorics on graphs, lattice theory, and finite group theory; each step is subtle and none can be bypassed by the direct modular trick that solves the original problem.
